item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article, across multiple sources, has not appeared to be released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed for being non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The connection between something as banal and everyday as sheets of A4 paper and something as fantastical as faster-than-light travel is unexpected but also, surprisingly, thematically consistent. This is juxtaposition at its finest/sexiest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like the format screw is being used as a crutch, to supplant the use of actual text to build up an atmosphere of loneliness.

The only thing I'd say in my defence is that actual text would have disqualified me from the Short Works contest for being over the word count!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think this piece works out for me. The FTL/Blank-Space connection made enough sense for me, but the article failed to engage me, or to invoke any sort of pathos. I feel like the format screw is being used as a crutch, to supplant the use of actual text to build up an atmosphere of loneliness. It's a shame, since the visual effect is actually rather good, and I want to see someone do justice to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I *wish* someone else had inserted all those spaces for me! Second-worst formatting job I've had to do on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I first read this, I though all the spaces in it where put in by someone who edited the article just as a prank. I checked the history, and all other edits had this. I then read the SCP, and I was corrected HARD.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
That is an absolutely excellent suggestion, MrWrong - exactly the sort of thing I was thinking of.

I would be happy with either an in-line link (perhaps on "faster-than-light interstellar vessel") or a new endnote noting the similarities between results of the missions, provided any new text didn't take this beyond the 500 word competition limit.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I'm trying to think of some better options - if anyone knows of SCPs involving the existential horror of an empty universe, feel free to speak up!

If 'empty universe' means a universe where humanity is alone and there is no signs of alien life, SCP-2669 can be used. 2669 is also about exploration into space, and finding no life out there.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
On its face, this is a clever link. But thinking about it more, I'm not sure it matches, either logistically or thematically. This skip is about going out into the dark and finding only emptiness. SCP-3200 is about going out and finding other universes, but that they can't be saved. In addition, linking from "nothingness" implies that Dr Harper didn't reach the edge of the universe, as claimed (just the Bootes void) which rather undermines the message.

I'm trying to think of some better options - if anyone knows of SCPs involving the existential horror of an empty universe, feel free to speak up!

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below (see MrWrong's comment). Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

While I was coming the wiki looking for a suitable crosslink for this article, A Random Day pointed out that both SCP-2161 and SCP-3200 deal with the empty spaces, both to a horror effect. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

WATCHED IT EXPAND INTO NOTHINGNESS

To this:

WATCHED IT EXPAND INTO NOTHINGNESS

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Congrats psul! It's well-deserved. :)

Just in time for the first anniversary of my joining the site, this one hits +100! Thanks to all you nice people who voted :)

Happy wiki-birthday to me, happy wiki-birthday to me…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice short piece.

I'm afraid that, in this case, there is no author head-canon hiding behind what's on the page. The switch from "blank paper" to "interstellar travel" is a pure writing device - I wanted (and needed, for wordcount reasons) to switch abruptly from the skip being about one thing to being about another.

It's really funny that the author didn't plan the connection, because it clicked for me perfectly. If I understand all those fun dumbed-down-for-laymen articles, in theoretical "warp"/"gravity-distortion" drives, you'd "move" "faster than light" by contracting space in front of you and, at the same time, expanding space behind you. That expansion part could be described like inflating a balloon upon which the universe is written - or adding spaces between every letter in a text.
It's very SCP for a device to literally have a metaphorical side-effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this one, though that ending note was a little too stylistic for me. I'd have preferred, say, a 'real' photograph of it, as that would have been more… I don't know, 'immersive' for me?

In all, I really liked this SCP. I think that ze Dr. is out using the FTL drive, yeah? And it made me smile when I realized that the pages had slowed down because he was no longer traveling as much.

Lovely~

Though I am curious as to the lack of a mention of the resilience of these pages. Can't they just be burned? Or did I miss something? (I just reread it and I don't think I missed anything, but I've had worse cases of 'blindness' before.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A great use of a very generic document icon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man, I'll be honest, I like WE ARE ALONE way better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But I feel like I'm missing a pretty crucial connector between "FTL drive used to explore the universe" and "blank spaces in documentation about it". That's a hell of a step to take with not even a hint of possible explanation …

Someone tell me to shut up if I'm wrong here, but I was under the impression that the "space" added to documents serves to instantaneously move the ship that much more distance, and that's how it all works

I'm afraid that, in this case, there is no author head-canon hiding behind what's on the page. The switch from "blank paper" to "interstellar travel" is a pure writing device - I wanted (and needed, for wordcount reasons) to switch abruptly from the skip being about one thing to being about another. Earlier drafts were even more jarring. It was a writing choice made with the knowledge that it would definitely not work for all readers - and weizhong (amongst others) made that pretty clear in feedback - but I wanted to see whether some people would go along with it.

Or, if you wanted to get all flowery about it - I'm asking the reader to make the leap into space at the same time as Dr Harper, but if you're out there looking for meaning, you're going to be searching a long time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems that it would need a hell of a lot more space than what's here in order to make it to the edge of the universe. And the causal connection still seems pretty tenuous there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Someone tell me to shut up if I'm wrong here, but I was under the impression that the "space" added to documents serves to instantaneously move the ship that much more distance, and that's how it all works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The work done in presenting this is admirable and comes down on the better side of the immersive/annoying divide when it comes to format screws. So that part was something I enjoyed.

The blank spaces being a side effect of FTL travel, though. I dunno. As always, I don't need or even want to know all the details of the thing in most cases. But I feel like I'm missing a pretty crucial connector between "FTL drive used to explore the universe" and "blank spaces in documentation about it". That's a hell of a step to take with not even a hint of possible explanation, and as a result it doesn't really feel like a finished piece to me. The concept itself is one that I can envision working, but the execution at present doesn't do it justice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've just realised, reading back through the discussion thread for the contest itself, that I definitely owe Guilliman some credit for this skip. I had completely forgotten by the time I wrote it, but I'm sure that this post was part of my inspiration.

[http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-1268714/short-works-contest#post-2331391]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is a great article, and I like the ending as is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the original version. Some may find it narmy, but I thought that the original closing line was great.

We need more "vast, uncaring universe" skips. Like, effective ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On a side-note, there's a translation of this over on the Chinese site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks all for the kind comments. Many thanks to Scantron, Dr Hysteria, Eta Carinae, Agent MacLeod, Dr Leonard, sirpudding, Decibelles, manofdoom and weizhong for encouragement and feedback on the draft.

I agree that the last line (and the whole final note) is difficult. I had many different versions, as those on the forums can attest, and this one was last minute, and arguably a change of tack.

I almost cut the whole note for a downer final addendum that noted that the creation of white space had slowed to a stop. On balance, I think I prefer Dr Harper still out there, searching.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cute, though "it's affecting the article" was a little predictable. You took it the extra step with that documentation, though, so upvote.

As at 07/01/2015

Did you mean "As of"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find this article really, really good. I would have liked it more if the last line ended with an ellipsis, but its a personal taste.

You are awesome, psul! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really really good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't much like the last line either - but I still love this piece to pieces and always will. As stated in the draft comments, a story about too much paper for a short works contest is a stroke of genius; and I picture that words on blank space = worlds in empty space, and this is even more genius.

+1. Good work, Psul!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pros:

  • The development of this article is interesting and I do like the buildup.
  • Interesting usage of blank space to incorporate your concepts in.

Cons:

  • I still think the last note is borderline cliche and reminds me of Doctor Who tropes.

Verdict: Upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ayup, this is pretty great. Although I personally preferred it when the last line was "WE ARE ALONE" - a little cliche, perhaps, but got the message across. But there's something a little optimistic about "I WILL SEARCH ON". Anyway, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved this in the draft stage, love it now. Blank space as propulsion residue is great, finding nothing at the edge of the universe is also great. More SCPs like this, please.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article is now open for review/voting/etc. Treat it as a normal article!

item.title: Author post
item.content:encoded:
As per the contest rules, this page allows staff to upload my entry, as I won't be around to do so during the submission period.

Until they do, everyone will just have to put up with blank space.

Please don't vote before the competition period starts!
